essence: David closes POEM story 4.3 (mock generator) and settles npm-link/developer-docs questions, but wants story 1.6's out-of-sequence completion committed with a callout and is unhappy that the visual design of stories 4.1-4.3 UI still looks bad despite previously requested changes.

actions.text: Commit story 1.6 plus documentation changes and add an out-of-sequence callout under epic one
Redo the visual design of stories 4.1-4.3 which did not improve after the earlier change request

essence: David onboards Angela onto Claude Code and the POEM system on her Windows/WSL machine — restructuring her dev folder into supportsignal/angela_poc, cloning the private prompt.supportsignal.com.au repo, explaining Penny/workflows/Handlebars — and they agree to recurring daily 1-hour training sessions from noon the next day.

actions.text: Run recurring daily 1-hour Angela training sessions starting tomorrow at noon
Angela to research and install a Windows voice dictation tool (e.g. Whisper Flow) for prompt writing

essence: David specs the VibeDeck (Vydave v1.0) retro web app — rotary dial that rotates and clicks, six context buttons across banks A-C (24 total), app-focus status dot, push-to-talk mic — asking for a Socket.IO backend with file-watching (FliHub/FliGen patterns) plus a small agent that generates ~20 alternative front-end variants from the same data.

actions.text: Build VibeDeck HTML front end plus Socket.IO backend with file watching and app-focus integration
Build an agent that generates ~20 alternative front-end design variants from the same data

essence: David, Ian, Nick and Martin brainstorm turning the Stream Deck vibe-coding workflow into a dedicated hardware console (rotary dial, LCD context buttons, big accept/reject/push-to-talk buttons) backed by a mandatory local software layer that routes to multiple LLM backends to stay within Claude Code TOS, weighing Kickstarter/open-source/Stream-Deck-profile strategies and agreeing on parallel solo software POCs before any hardware spend.

actions.text: Each member builds a solo software proof-of-concept / UX variant of the vibe deck, then reconvene to merge best ideas
Design the local software shim layer (JSON-configurable, multi-backend: Claude/Gemini/OpenAI/Codex) rather than talking directly to Claude Code

essence: David asks for paste-ready handover instructions so a fresh Claude Code session can resume mid-story work with the BMAD 'James the developer' agent via the AppyDave workflow, planning to restart around 12:30 after coffee.

Nearing the context-window limit with state locked in by a commit, David decides the next-session sequence — explore the button options first (option 3), then have Penny generate the prompts (option 1), then implement (option 2) — delivered as a conversational handover rather than a document.

actions.text: Produce a conversational handover encoding the option 3 -> 1 -> 2 sequence for the next session

essence: David transitions the vibe-coding deck work from the documentation phase into the fixing phase, supplying a set of guide images and new information to correct the broken slides, and asks whether any vibe-coding Stream Deck profiles already exist.

actions.text: Fix the broken deck slides using the newly supplied information and guide images

essence: David feeds in fresh ChatGPT research, flags that implementation 3.7 was done incorrectly and asks Claude to determine recovery (pure dev fix vs revisiting story context, including the deleted 'raw vibe deck' notes), confirms the vibe deck as the first Nano Banana generation project, and approves option A.

actions.text: Work out how to recover from the incorrect implementation 3.7 — dev-only fix or revisit story/context information

essence: David, frustrated the agent lost the session-five handover conversation it wrote, demands a short pasteable handover referencing planning/PRD/architecture files, asks to see the output of the two schemas, and queries internal terminology ('render of the vibes for Shane', 'handle driven iteration').

David corrects a core misimplementation: a prompt was never meant to have a dual schema FILE system — it is one prompt with an input schema and an output schema (function/C#-tuple analogy, outputs can be string, meta-object, or list) mapping onto workflow attributes — and instructs the agent to stop acting and research the correct schemas from the planning docs/PRD.

actions.text: Do not act; research and explicitly define the correct input and output schema for the prompt from planning docs, architecture and PRD

essence: David sets a two-step Nano Banana research workflow — step one organize raw data (the 'nano prompt' and 'raw vibe deck' research files) with transient notes, step two define schemas/prompts — and decides to test dual-schema API behavior via the existing generate-titles prompt instead of creating a new one, deprioritizing Gemini/Sora comparisons.

actions.text: Organize raw Nano Banana research data with transient notes before defining any schemas or prompts
Test dual-schema API behavior using the existing generate-titles prompt

essence: David commissions deep research into what context engineers and vibe coders actually do with BMAD/Spec Kit/OpenSpec — three summaries by persona, workflow stage, and toolchain — to derive the buttons/sliders/context-switch/feedback mechanisms a vibe-coding deck needs, plus an Epic 3 rundown (data folder as source of truth vs dev workspaces) and raw-data consolidation for an upcoming Nano Banana image-generation prompt system.

actions.text: Run deep research on context-engineering workflows and produce three summaries: by persona, by workflow stage, by toolchain
Consolidate raw data and create asset ideas for the Nano Banana image-generation prompt system data feed
